A History of Mind

Renaissance Early Modern

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Jakob von Uexküll

1864 – 1944

Umwelt, organism, and mind as a world-shaping relation.

Jakob von Uexküll’s thought can be reconstructed as a rigorous philosophy of mind, but only if “mind” is not reduced to private introspection, brain states, linguistic intelligence, or human self-consciousness. Its central claim is that a living being is a subject that does not first encounter a neutral physical world and then add “mental” color to it. Rather, the living subject selects signs, forms objects, acts through meaningful cues, and inhabits an Umwelt: a species- and individual-specific world of perceptual and practical significance. Uexküll’s philosophy of mind is therefore a philosophy of embodied, world-forming, semiotic subjectivity.

The most compact formulation is this: mind, for Uexküll, is the living subject’s organized capacity to transform surroundings into a meaningful world of perception and action. Consciousness is only one illuminated region of that organization. “Soul” names the domain of thoughts and feelings, but Uexküll does not treat it as a detachable Cartesian substance. “Spirit” or Geist, especially in his late theological writings, names a still higher formal or melodic principle that is not reducible to the cerebral “instrument.” Theology is not incidental to this picture, because his mature view of life is built around composition, melody, plan, counterpoint, and a universal score. The theological claims are not always required by the empirical Umwelt theory, but they disclose the metaphysical direction in which Uexküll himself pushes it.

1. Corpus, method, and interpretive caution

The central texts for this reconstruction are Theoretical Biology; A Stroll Through the Worlds of Animals and Men; The Theory of Meaning; and, more cautiously, the late German dialogue Der unsterbliche Geist in der Natur / The Immortal Spirit in Nature. The latter is harder to consult in full online, so it should be used with methodological restraint; available German bibliographic and review material nevertheless makes clear that Uexküll explicitly connected the brain, spirit, melody, and immortality in that work. A German bookseller record identifies Der unsterbliche Geist in der Natur. Gespräche as a 1938 work by Jakob von Uexküll, and an accessible German review excerpt reports Uexküll’s concluding analogy: as a melody outlasts the barrel-organ, human spirit outlasts the cerebrum.

Uexküll’s own methodological starting point is deeply Kantian. He says in Theoretical Biology that qualities are signs and that there is no meaningful scientific route to a reality “behind” appearances; he famously writes that “all reality is subjective appearance.” But this is not crude idealism, and it is not a denial of living bodies. His point is that biology must extend the Kantian critique of experience to nonhuman subjects by asking how their bodies, sense organs, nervous systems, and action systems constitute worlds.

This matters because the common Anglo-American reception sometimes flattens Uexküll into a precursor of “affordances,” “enactivism,” “biosemiotics,” or animal phenomenology. Those comparisons are useful, but incomplete. French, Spanish, and German receptions often preserve more of his Kantian, metaphysical, anti-mechanistic, and theological dimensions. A French study explicitly situates his concepts of subject, sign, space, and time between Descartes, Kant, Berkeley, Peirce, Heidegger, and Merleau-Ponty; Spanish discussions emphasize the shift from perception to behavior, the heterogeneity of lived animal temporalities, and the unresolved problem of chance or transgression within Uexküll’s planned order; older German Catholic reception read the Umwelt doctrine as having direct metaphysical and even soul-theoretical significance.

2. The basic architecture: subject, Umwelt, signs, and functional circle

Uexküll’s key move is to replace the observer’s external world with the animal’s own world. In A Stroll Through the Worlds of Animals and Men, he asks the reader to enter unfamiliar animal worlds, comparing them to “soap bubbles” surrounding each subject. The point is not that animals live in imaginary worlds. It is that each organism’s world is structured by what can count for it as a perceptual sign and by what it can do in response.

The Umwelt is the closed unity of a perceptual world and an effector or action world. Everything a subject perceives becomes part of its perceptual world; everything it does becomes part of its effector world; the two together form its Umwelt. This is why Uexküll rejects the mechanist’s picture of animals as merely reactive machines. For the biologist, an individual animal must be treated as a subject, a center of its own world, not as a mechanical device inserted into a single neutral environment.

The central technical structure is the functional circle, or Funktionskreis. An object becomes meaningful for an organism only insofar as it enters a circular relation between perceptual cue and action cue. In The Theory of Meaning, Uexküll’s famous flower-stem example shows that the same physical stem can be ornament for a human, path for an ant, extraction-point for a larva, or fodder for a cow. The object does not possess one universal meaning; it becomes a meaning-carrier relative to a subject’s capacities and tasks.

This is not a minor biological observation. It is the basis of a philosophy of intentionality. For Uexküll, intentional content is not first an inner representation of an outer object. It is a subject-object relation in which the object is already encountered as usable, avoidable, edible, dangerous, sheltering, mate-like, prey-like, path-like, and so on. A paving stone, for example, can be part of a path or become a projectile depending on the subject’s comportment and mood. Meaning is not added afterward to a bare object; the object that matters is already constituted as meaningful.

This is why Uexküll’s theory is close to, but stronger than, later theories of affordance. An affordance account says that organisms perceive possibilities for action. Uexküll says something more radical: the organism’s world is composed of such meaningful cues. The same surroundings are not merely perceived differently; they are different Umwelten. French reception captures this well when it describes Uexküll’s “actantial” world as a subjective space oriented toward action, in which perceptual and action worlds are connected through a feedback-like functional circle.

3. The subject: biological subjectivity before self-consciousness

Uexküll’s “subject” is not limited to the reflective human ego. This is one of his most important contributions to philosophy of mind. A subject is a living center of selection, perception, action, and world-formation. French interpreters have rightly noted that Uexküll’s move from Cartesian to Kantian subjectivity frees the subject from the requirement of explicit self-consciousness; he then applies this broadened subjectivity to living beings generally.

In Theoretical Biology, Uexküll distinguishes a biological subject from a psychological subject. The biological subject is associated with the autonomous rule of function in a living whole. A fully formed animal is a subject in this biological sense; even an egg-cell can be considered in relation to subject-like developmental organization, though he qualifies the status of early embryonic phases where no unified functional rule is yet evident.

The psychological subject, by contrast, is the ego: the unity of apperception and the source of ordered impulses. But Uexküll insists that biology cannot directly enter another subject’s apperceptive center. Biology can observe outward effects, functional organization, reactions, and action patterns; it cannot literally occupy the animal’s inner stage.

This yields a layered theory of mind. At the broadest level, subjectivity belongs to life as organized world-relation. At a narrower level, psychological subjectivity belongs to egoic unity, apperception, and the ordering of impulses. At a still narrower level, consciousness is the appearance of signs, qualities, and schemata on what Uexküll calls the “stage” of consciousness. Human reflective self-consciousness is not the model for all mind; it is a special case within a larger biological theory of subjectivity.

A useful reconstruction is:

Level Uexküllian role Philosophy-of-mind equivalent Living subject Center of perception-action organization Minimal biological subjectivity Umwelt Subject-specific meaningful world Intentional world / lived world Functional circle Coupling of perceptual cue and action cue Enactive-semiotic intentionality Inner world / Innenwelt Hidden organization of impulses, schemata, and bodily functions Preconscious or unconscious organization Consciousness-stage Where signs, qualities, and schemata appear Phenomenal presentation Ego / psychological subject Unity of apperception and ordered action Selfhood / first-person unity Spirit / Geist Formal, melodic, higher principle of meaning Personal or metaphysical mind

This table is a reconstruction, not Uexküll’s own systematic taxonomy. But it makes explicit what is distributed across his texts.

4. Kant biologized: all reality as subjectively constituted appearance

The foundation of Uexküll’s philosophy of mind is his biological transformation of Kant. Kant had argued that human experience is structured by forms and categories of the knowing subject. Uexküll radicalizes this by asking: what are the forms of experience for a tick, dog, fly, sea urchin, or human? Biology, on this view, becomes a comparative critique of possible animal worlds.

In Theoretical Biology, Uexküll says that Kant’s theory must be expanded because the subject should not be treated as a purely abstract knower. The body, sense organs, central nervous system, and even nonhuman subjects must be brought into the analysis. The “subject,” for Uexküll, includes mental and spiritual powers but is also biologically embodied.

This is not epistemological relativism in the loose sense. Uexküll is not saying that organisms arbitrarily invent worlds. Their worlds are rigorously constrained by their bodily organization, sense organs, action possibilities, developmental melodies, and species-specific plans. But the “object” as encountered is always a product of relation. Place, for example, is not simply there in the physical environment; the animal’s place mosaic is given by the subject.

The same point applies to individuality. Uexküll says no two human Umwelten are exactly the same. Even a familiar path becomes familiar through the individual subject’s own lived organization. Territory, similarly, is not merely an objective area of land; it is a subjective product of Umwelt formation.

The philosophical consequence is significant: Uexküll gives us a theory of objectivity without a view from nowhere. Objectivity is not denied; it is relocated. A thing becomes objective for a subject when it is stabilized through local signs, perceptual cues, action possibilities, and repeatable functional relations. There may be scientific comparison among Umwelten, but no organism, including the human scientist, escapes Umwelt altogether.

5. Body and soul: not Cartesian dualism, not reductionism

Uexküll is anti-mechanistic, but he is not a simple substance dualist. He does not say that there is a material body here and a separate immaterial soul there. Instead, he treats body and mind as two correlated expressions of organized life.

In Theoretical Biology, he says that organization applies to body and mind alike, and that every organ of the body corresponds to an organ of the mind. If a bodily organ is suppressed, the corresponding mental activity is suppressed too. This is not reductionism, because he does not reduce mental organization to matter; but it is also not dualism, because mental activity is inseparable from the living bodily organization through which an Umwelt is constituted.

His discussion of soul is especially subtle. In a thought experiment concerning local signs, he argues that without localization, sense qualities would remain merely properties of the subject; thoughts, feelings, and perceptions would not yet be sharply distinguished. Once local signs are introduced, perceptions become objectified as belonging to things, while thoughts and feelings remain grouped as “soul.”

This means that the body-soul opposition is partly produced by the structure of objectification. The body becomes an object in the subject’s world because it can be localized; the soul appears as non-objective because thoughts and feelings do not normally carry the same local signs. Uexküll even says that the contrast between body and soul would disappear if soul-events had local signs in the same way bodily perceptions do. He also notes that feelings are not wholly non-spatial, because they may be connected with directional signs.

This is one of the most important passages for extracting a philosophy of mind from Uexküll. It suggests that “soul” is not an independent metaphysical container. It is the name given, from within a subject’s constituted world, to thoughts and feelings that are not objectified as localized external things. The soul/body contrast is therefore a phenomenological and semiotic distinction, not simply a metaphysical division between two substances.

Yet Uexküll does not reduce the soul to neural mechanisms. His claim is closer to a hylomorphic or formal view: living organization has both bodily and mental aspects, and the body is not intelligible as a machine unless one ignores the subject-centered plan that animates its functions.

6. Consciousness: the stage, the threshold, and the hidden work of mind

Uexküll’s theory of consciousness is not the whole of his theory of mind. Consciousness is a partial manifestation of deeper subjective organization.

He uses a theatrical metaphor: schemata appear on a stage of consciousness, dressed in sensory qualities. But the construction of objects depends on a mental process “of which we know nothing,” a hidden art that emerges from the unconscious. He explicitly invokes Kant’s idea of a hidden art in the depths of the soul, and he compares memory to theatre machinery that brings schemata onto the stage.

This should not be misread as a Cartesian theatre theory in the modern pejorative sense. Uexküll is not positing a little spectator inside the head. The metaphor’s real function is to distinguish visible conscious appearance from hidden formative processes. What consciousness presents is not the origin of cognition; it is the staged result of prior schematization, memory, bodily organization, and impulse.

He is equally clear that action is not transparent to consciousness. Schemata are acquired through movement, but the impulse-sequences directing activity are not directly experienced. We know our actions through sensory signs; we do not know the impulse-system itself in its own intrinsic character.

This becomes even sharper in his discussion of will. Uexküll says there is no proper “Critique of Will-Power” because we know so little about will-power itself. The command contained in impulses evades consciousness; the architect or director of the impulse-system remains hidden. He even states that the transcendental subject is wider than spirit, since spirit embraces ego-life while the deeper subject includes hidden impulse-organization.

This yields a highly modern-sounding but distinctively Uexküllian view: consciousness is not the sovereign source of action. Conscious life is a thresholded presentation within a larger living system. Much of the subject’s world-forming activity occurs below conscious awareness, in bodily schemata, perceptual selection, action-readiness, and functional circles.

7. The animal mind and the problem of other consciousnesses

Uexküll’s animal philosophy is both generous and austere. It is generous because it grants animals subjecthood. It is austere because it denies that we can simply project our own qualities into them.

When investigating animals, he says, we can never directly know their sensations. We can only identify the indications in the sensed world to which they react, and then infer their perceptual organs and Umwelt structures.

This is his answer to the problem of other minds. We do not know another organism’s consciousness by introspective transfer. We reconstruct it through the organism’s functional circle: what it notices, what it ignores, what actions it performs, which cues matter, which cues do not, and how its bodily organization limits or opens a world. In another passage, Uexküll says the outside observer cannot state an animal’s qualities without getting inside the animal’s mind; since that is impossible, the observer must deduce perceptual organs from the indications to which animals react.

This produces a disciplined comparative phenomenology. Uexküll does not say “the tick feels as we would feel in its place.” He says: ask what counts as a sign for the tick. In the famous tick case, the relevant world is dramatically reduced: the tick waits, responds to butyric acid, warmth, and tactile cues, and thereby lives in an Umwelt radically different from ours. Uexküll uses the example to contrast the mechanist, who treats the animal as an object, with the biologist, who treats it as a subject whose stimulus must first be perceived as a sign.

The result is a position that should be called biological pan-subjectivism rather than panpsychism in the ordinary contemporary sense. Uexküll does not straightforwardly claim that all matter is conscious. He claims that living beings are centers of world-formation. He extends subjectivity downward into life, but he also distinguishes biological subjectivity from psychological ego-consciousness and refuses to explain other organisms by projecting our own psychical experiences onto them.

8. Perception, action, and “functional tone”

Uexküll’s account of mind is inherently practical. Organisms do not merely receive sensory data; they encounter things under functional tones.

In A Stroll, he explains that we often do not look for a particular chair as a neutral object; we look for “something to sit on.” The same image can receive different tones: protective tone, dwelling tone, eating tone, and so forth.

Functional tone is a crucial bridge between perception and valuation. It means that the world appears already charged with practical significance. A stone can appear as obstacle, weapon, path-component, or building material. A flower can appear as ornament, food, landing site, or reproductive partner depending on the subject. A prey animal, a mate, and a predator are not neutral physical bodies later interpreted; they are encountered as meaning-bearing Gestalten.

In philosophy-of-mind terms, Uexküll is denying the priority of sense-data. Perception is not the passive reception of atomistic sensory contents. It is selective, action-guided, value-laden, and species-specific. This is why his work can be fruitfully compared with phenomenology, ecological psychology, enactivism, and biosemiotics, but it should not be collapsed into any one of them.

Spanish scholarship has emphasized that Uexküll’s Umwelt concept shifts over time from a primarily perceptual problem toward a broader theory of behavior: subject and object are not isolated terms but interrelated components of a coherent system. That interpretive emphasis is important because it prevents us from treating Umwelt as merely “what the animal sees.” Umwelt is what the animal can meaningfully perceive-and-do.

9. Space, time, and objecthood as subject-dependent forms

Uexküll’s philosophy of mind includes an implicit philosophy of space and time. Space is not simply an empty container in which organisms are placed. The organism’s world has its own place-system, distance-system, directionality, and action-relevant layout.

His discussion of local signs shows that spatial objectivity is subject-dependent. A thing becomes an object for a subject when qualities are localized and stabilized. Without local signs, qualities would remain attached to the subject rather than distributed into an external world.

The same is true of time. Different organisms inhabit different temporal rhythms. Spanish reception has emphasized the heterogeneity of lived temporal structures in animal Umwelten, a point that follows naturally from Uexküll’s insistence that each subject has its own world of perception and action.

This gives Uexküll a place in the history of transcendental philosophy. Kant asked how human space and time are possible. Uexküll asks how multiple biological spaces and times are possible. His answer is not that each organism consciously constructs space and time, but that the living subject’s bodily organization, perceptual signs, and action systems articulate a world in which things can appear as near, far, reachable, dangerous, delayed, rhythmic, familiar, territorial, and so on.

10. Imagination, innate melody, and “magic” Umwelten

Uexküll’s theory also includes phenomena that today might be discussed under imagination, instinct, inherited action-patterns, and virtual objects.

In A Stroll, he discusses “magic Umwelten,” including cases where an animal’s action is guided by signs that are not simply present as ordinary external stimuli. The starling’s “imaginary fly” and the inborn path or melody of action suggest that some Umwelt structures are not learned from external objects but arise from inherited melodic organization.

This is an important addition to the philosophy of mind. Uexküll is not only a theorist of perception. He is also a theorist of anticipatory organization. The animal’s world is structured by expectancies, action-readiness, innate schemas, and species-specific paths. Perception is already prepared by a form of life.

That said, this is also where Uexküll’s biology must be handled critically. His vocabulary of inherited melody, plan, and impulse does not map directly onto contemporary genetics or neuroscience. The durable philosophical point is not that his specific biological mechanisms were correct, but that mind is organized by pre-reflective form, not merely assembled from present sensory input.

11. Development, organism, and the “plan” of life

Uexküll’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his theory of organism. He contrasts organisms with machines. Machines perform functions because external builders assembled them; organisms develop through genesis and function according to a plan-like organization internal to life. He argues that organisms appear to contain both actual natural factors and a plan that cannot be reduced to mechanism.

In Theoretical Biology, he speaks of universal conformity with plan and of subjective impulse-systems woven into objective natural factors. Impulses form world-centers and organize protoplasm, though he admits that the exact way this occurs is unknown.

This is one of the points at which Uexküll’s theory becomes metaphysical. The living subject is not merely a point of view; it is the center of a formative organization. The Umwelt is not a subjective overlay on an independently complete object-world. It is correlated with the organism’s own bodily plan. The organism and its Umwelt belong together.

In The Theory of Meaning, this becomes the doctrine of melody and counterpoint. Living cells are compared to a chime; sense organs select stimuli; brain-cell “chimes” receive signs; effector organs translate ego-qualities into impulses. Receptor and effector organs form counterpoints. Development itself begins from a single cell attuned to a developmental melody.

This is not just poetic language. It is Uexküll’s alternative to mechanism. A living being is not a machine that happens to have experiences. It is a score-like organization whose bodily structures, perceptual signs, actions, and Umwelt meanings cohere according to a form. His musical metaphors are therefore philosophically central.

12. Meaning as the essence of life

The Theory of Meaning is indispensable for a philosophy of mind because it shows that Uexküll’s final concern is not perception alone but meaning. He argues that meaningful objects enter into an organism’s world while meaningless physical properties fall away. From the point of view of a house, for example, the garden is not simply a segment of a larger physical world; only meaningful objects enter a given Umwelt. The same object assumes different properties in different Umwelten because properties are perceptual cues imprinted by the subject.

This makes Uexküll one of the great anti-reductionist theorists of meaning in biology. Meaning is not a human linguistic luxury. It is present wherever a living subject distinguishes relevant from irrelevant, sign from noise, cue from background, object from surroundings, and action from mere movement.

The result is a biosemiotic philosophy of mind. Signs are not merely conventional symbols. A sign is any cue that functions within the organism’s perceptual-action system. A smell, warmth gradient, pressure, color, contour, rhythm, or movement can be a sign if it enters the subject’s functional circle. Consciousness, where present, is a manifestation of sign-life; it is not the origin of sign-life.

This also has consequences for emotion and value. Uexküll does not offer a modern theory of affect, but his functional tones imply that affective salience is built into world-formation. To perceive something as prey, danger, mate, shelter, obstacle, or tool is already to inhabit a value-structured world.

13. Human mind: extension without escape

Humans are unusual for Uexküll, but not exempt from Umwelt. He says that humans can broaden and deepen their Umwelt with perceptual aids and effector tools, but cannot go beyond the perimeter set by human nature. Scientific instruments enlarge the human world; they do not give us the view of God.

This is a crucial correction to naïve scientism. Uexküll does not reject science; he practices it. But he denies that science is a disembodied access to reality as it is apart from all possible subjectivity. Scientific objectivity is an achievement inside the human Umwelt, aided by instruments, mathematics, experiment, and intersubjective correction.

The human mind is therefore distinguished by its capacity to compare Umwelten, construct models, extend perception, and recognize the polyphony of living worlds. But this very recognition remains humanly mediated. We may infer the tick’s world, the bee’s world, or the dog’s world, but we do not simply become those subjects.

This gives Uexküll’s theory ethical and epistemic humility. The human is not the measure of all animals. Nor is the human scientist outside the system. Humans are one voice in a broader composition.

14. Theology I: composition, master plan, and God

The theological dimension of Uexküll’s thought is not an accidental ornament. It emerges directly from his mature language of melody, score, counterpoint, and universal composition.

In The Theory of Meaning, he asks us to imagine the harmony of animal worlds almost as one would listen to a great musical work. He denies that animal life should be understood through progressivist hierarchies or survivalist reduction. Each animal fills its stage with meaningful objects and fellow actors; properties harmonize like point and counterpoint.

He then moves explicitly into theological language. Against the idea that life is governed merely by struggle, progress, and the survival of the fittest, he speaks of the “master’s hand,” compositions, melodies, and meaning as that which rules all. He invokes Leopold von Ranke’s theological claim that every epoch is close to God, then extends it biologically: each living group’s Umwelten can be traced to God insofar as all Umwelten belong to the same work of composition.

This theological move serves several functions.

First, it rejects reductionism. If the world of life is a composition, organisms cannot be adequately explained as machines or aggregates of matter.

Second, it rejects anthropocentric progressivism. A tick, sea urchin, bee, dog, and human are not simply lower and higher rungs on a single ladder. Each has its own completeness within its Umwelt.

Third, it gives ontological dignity to meaning. Meaning is not a subjective illusion floating over brute matter. Meaning is woven into the very structure of living reality.

Fourth, it offers a metaphysical account of plurality. The many Umwelten are not chaos; they are voices in one score.

Still, caution is necessary. Uexküll’s theological language is not always equivalent to classical doctrinal theology. Sometimes “God” functions as the name for the composer of the universal score. Sometimes his position is closer to objective teleology or formal metaphysics. And in Theoretical Biology, he explicitly resists appealing to a vague universal world-intelligence; he prefers to speak of concrete natural factors, impulse-systems, and plan-conformity rather than dissolving psychical boundaries into a cosmic mind.

So the right reading is not: Uexküll secretly turns biology into simple theology. Rather: his biology of meaning is structurally open to a theology of composition, and in some late texts he embraces that opening explicitly.

15. Theology II: spirit, brain, melody, and immortality

The late work Der unsterbliche Geist in der Natur pushes the metaphysical and theological line further. The accessible German review excerpt reports that Uexküll treats the brain as a kind of three-dimensional score of the human spirit and concludes with the analogy that the melody outlasts the barrel-organ just as human spirit outlasts the cerebrum.

This should not be dismissed as merely an old biologist’s pious flourish. It is continuous with his whole metaphysics of melody. If organisms are not machines but score-like formations, then the material instrument is not identical with the formal-melodic principle that animates it. The brain may be necessary for the manifestation of human spirit within embodied life, but the spirit is not simply the brain considered as matter.

A secondary source on Uexküll’s late thought notes that in The Immortal Spirit of Nature he discusses different kinds of “thought webs” or Denkgewebe, including world-structures with multiple fixed reference points, one fixed point, or none. Contemporary German-language scholarship has also continued to treat this late work as relevant to philosophy of mind: a 2025 peer-reviewed German chapter explicitly reads Der unsterbliche Geist in der Natur in relation to current “4E” concepts of the spiritual or mental.

The theological thesis can be reconstructed as follows. In ordinary embodied cognition, spirit appears through cerebral, sensory, and motor organization. But the form of spirit is not exhausted by that organization, just as a melody is not exhausted by the physical device that plays it. The analogy is not a proof in the contemporary analytic sense. It is a metaphysical argument from form: if living meaning is essentially melodic, then the bearer of the melody is not identical with the material instrument.

This is the point at which Uexküll’s philosophy of mind becomes most contestable. His empirical Umwelt theory does not by itself demonstrate immortality. It supports anti-reductionism, formal organization, and the irreducibility of meaning to mechanism. The stronger claim that human spirit survives the cerebrum requires an additional metaphysical-theological premise: that form or melody can subsist beyond its embodied performance. That premise is coherent within his musical metaphysics, but it is not forced by his animal phenomenology alone.

16. The extracted philosophy of mind: twelve theses

A comprehensive Uexküllian philosophy of mind can be stated in twelve theses.

First, mind is world-forming life. A mind is not primarily an inner container of representations. It is a living subject’s capacity to constitute an Umwelt through perception, action, signs, and meaning.

Second, subjectivity precedes self-consciousness. A living being can be a subject without being a reflective ego. Biological subjectivity is broader than psychological self-awareness.

Third, perception is selective sign-reception. Organisms do not receive the total physical environment. They select cues that matter for their functional circles.

Fourth, action is constitutive of experience. The perceptual world and effector world form a unity. What a subject can do helps determine what it can encounter.

Fifth, meaning is relational but real. Meaning does not lie in the object alone or in the subject alone. It arises in the subject-object relation, where an object becomes a meaning-carrier for a meaning-utilizer.

Sixth, consciousness is staged manifestation, not the whole mind. Conscious experience presents schemata, qualities, and signs, but the formative work of mind is often hidden, unconscious, bodily, and impulse-driven.

Seventh, the soul is not a detachable ghost. “Soul” names the domain of thoughts, feelings, and non-objectified experience, but the soul/body contrast itself depends on the structure of localization and objectification.

Eighth, the ego is not sovereign over will. The commands of impulse and the organization of action are not transparent to introspection. Uexküll’s subject includes hidden depths wider than conscious ego-life.

Ninth, other minds are inferred through worlds. We know animal minds by reconstructing their Umwelten, not by anthropomorphic projection or by reducing them to mechanisms.

Tenth, human cognition extends Umwelt but does not abolish it. Instruments, science, and concepts enlarge human access, but humans remain embodied subjects with species-specific forms.

Eleventh, life is musical or compositional. Organisms and Umwelten are not aggregates but patterned, counterpointed, plan-like wholes.

Twelfth, theology names the ultimate unity of meaning. In Uexküll’s mature thought, the plurality of Umwelten is not meaningless fragmentation but a universal score, whose composer he is willing to call God.

17. Comparison with contemporary philosophy of mind

Uexküll anticipates several contemporary positions, but he is identical with none of them.

He anticipates enactivism because he treats perception and action as inseparable. Mind is not passive representation but active world-relation.

He anticipates ecological psychology because objects are encountered through action-relevant meanings. But unlike a purely ecological account, Uexküll emphasizes the subjectively enclosed Umwelt and the species-specific semiotic constitution of objects.

He anticipates biosemiotics because he treats signs as fundamental to life. Meaning is not confined to language or human culture.

He anticipates phenomenology because he studies the world as it appears to a subject. But he biologizes phenomenology by extending it to nonhuman organisms and by grounding it in anatomical and behavioral study.

He anticipates embodied cognition because the body is not a vehicle for mind but part of the structure that makes a world possible.

He anticipates predictive or schema-based theories in a loose sense because perception depends on hidden schemata, memory, and anticipatory organization. But he does not offer a computational theory of prediction.

He differs from reductive physicalism because he does not treat consciousness and meaning as byproducts of matter in motion.

He differs from Cartesian dualism because he does not isolate soul from bodily organization.

He differs from panpsychism because he does not attribute consciousness to matter as such; he attributes subject-centered world-formation to living organization.

He differs from idealism in the purely mentalist sense because organisms, bodies, organs, and action systems are indispensable to his account.

The closest label might be biological transcendental semiotic realism. “Biological” because the subject is living and embodied. “Transcendental” because the organism’s forms condition its world. “Semiotic” because signs and meanings are basic. “Realism” because Umwelten are not arbitrary fantasies; they are real worlds for real organisms, though not the one absolute world of naïve realism.

18. Theological and metaphysical interpretation: hylomorphism, not mere vitalism

Uexküll is often called a vitalist, and there is truth in that label if it means he rejects mechanism and insists on irreducible life-organization. But “vitalism” can mislead if it suggests a mysterious extra force simply added to matter. His mature view is better understood as formal, musical, or hylomorphic.

On a hylomorphic reading, living beings are matter organized by form. Uexküll’s “melody” is not another material part; it is the ordering principle by which parts belong to a living whole. The body is the instrument, but the life-form is the score-like organization that makes the body a meaningful unity.

This reading is supported by older German metaphysical reception. Alois Dempf, writing in Philosophisches Jahrbuch, saw Uexküll’s Umwelt doctrine as relevant to the relation between nature and soul and argued that it leads from biology toward metaphysics, including questions of visible and invisible form, inner life, and classical morphology. He also noted that Theoretical Biology itself deliberately renounces metaphysics in a Kantian way, which is important: the metaphysical reading extends Uexküll, but it does not erase his methodological restraint.

This is the best way to handle Uexküll’s theology. His empirical biology gives us subjectivity, signs, functional circles, Umwelten, and plan-like organization. His metaphysics interprets these as melodies within a universal composition. His theology names the composer God. His immortality claim treats human spirit as melody not exhausted by cerebral machinery.

Whether one accepts the theology or not, it is not a distraction from his philosophy of mind. It clarifies what kind of anti-reductionism he means. He is not merely saying that consciousness is hard to explain. He is saying that life and mind are formally organized meanings whose intelligibility exceeds mechanical causation.

19. Critique: where Uexküll must be revised

A rigorous report should not merely admire Uexküll. Several tensions need to be made explicit.

First, his theory risks solipsistic closure. If every organism lives in its own soap-bubble world, how do worlds communicate, overlap, conflict, or correct one another? Uexküll has resources for answering this through functional relations and scientific comparison, but the image of closed Umwelten can overstate isolation. Recent work on Uexküll notes this “bubble” problem and connects it to later debates in philosophical anthropology and enactivism.

Second, his doctrine of harmony can underplay maladaptation, pathology, violence, contingency, and failure. Plessner’s critique is important here: organism and environment do not simply fit in perfect harmony; life involves danger, risk, openness, and constant correction.

Third, his anti-Darwinian polemic is too strong. Uexküll is right that life cannot be understood only as mechanical struggle or linear progress. But evolutionary contingency, selection, developmental plasticity, ecological instability, and failure must be integrated into any contemporary version of his theory. Spanish commentary has also emphasized that Uexküll’s world of plan and order leaves unresolved the role of chance, error, intention, and transgression.

Fourth, his account of consciousness lacks a detailed explanation of phenomenal emergence. He tells us how signs, schemata, and qualities appear on the consciousness-stage, and he rightly emphasizes hidden processes. But he does not give a contemporary account of why or how phenomenal consciousness arises from living organization.

Fifth, his theological inference to immortality is not entailed by the Umwelt theory. The melody/instrument analogy is philosophically suggestive, but it is not demonstrative unless one already accepts a metaphysics in which form can subsist beyond material performance.

Sixth, his language of plan can sound too static. Later interpreters such as Merleau-Ponty found in Uexküll a theory of living plan without necessarily accepting transcendent finality, while Plessner and Buytendijk criticized the rigidity of the perceptual-inner-effector world scheme for not doing justice to variability and changes of meaning.

A contemporary Uexküllian philosophy of mind should therefore preserve his subject-world semiotics while revising his overly harmonious and anti-evolutionary tendencies. The strongest updated version would combine Umwelt theory with developmental systems theory, enactivism, ecological psychology, affect theory, comparative cognition, and a more dynamic account of inter-Umwelt conflict and transformation.

20. Final synthesis

Jakob von Uexküll’s philosophy of mind can be stated as follows.

A living being is a subject. A subject does not inhabit a neutral physical environment but an Umwelt, a meaningful world constituted through the relation between perceptual signs and possible actions. Mind is not first a private inner theatre; it is the living organization that makes such a world possible. Consciousness is the stage on which some signs, qualities, and schemata appear, but the deeper work of world-formation occurs through bodily organs, hidden schemata, memory, impulse-systems, developmental melodies, and functional circles. Soul is the domain of thoughts and feelings as they appear within this subject-world, but it is not a separable ghostly substance. Spirit is the higher formal or melodic organization of personal meaning, and in Uexküll’s late theological thought it is not reducible to the brain.

Theologically, Uexküll’s world is a polyphonic composition. Each organism’s Umwelt is a finite, meaningful world; none is simply a defective version of the human world. The plurality of living worlds is not meaningless fragmentation but counterpoint. God, in Uexküll’s mature vocabulary, is the name for the composer of the universal score. That theological claim is not necessary for every application of Umwelt theory, but it is necessary for understanding the full ambition of Uexküll’s own thought.

The resulting philosophy of mind is embodied, semiotic, pluralist, anti-reductionist, and teleological. It is not a philosophy in which mind represents the world from inside the skull. It is a philosophy in which life itself, wherever it becomes a center of signs and actions, composes a world.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
